CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (content-selection + ILP + crowdsourcing) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel semi-automatic framework and DBPNLG dataset). Notation and methodological descriptions seem consistent and sufficiently detailed for reproduction (ILP formulation, bigram models, reported hyperparameters), and the evaluation uses a broad, appropriate set of metrics. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the related work coverage is thorough; a few presentation polish items remain (minor clarity improvements possible), but overall the writing is good and unambiguous. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through methodology, crowdsourcing pipeline, experiments and conclusion; main contributions are stated early and supported by detailed methodology and metrics. Notation appears consistent and the range of quantitative and qualitative metrics (attributes, input shapes, lexical measures, perplexity/BLEU) together with examples and comparisons to RNNLG make results easy to follow. Presentation is generally strong with good writing and thorough related work, though a few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., more explicit mention of code availability and clearer labeling of some figures/tables would improve reproducibility and reader navigation).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (content-selection + ILP + crowdsourcing) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel semi-automatic framework and DBPNLG dataset). Notation and methodological descriptions seem consistent and sufficiently detailed for reproduction (ILP formulation, bigram models, reported hyperparameters), and the evaluation uses a broad, appropriate set of metrics. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the related work coverage is thorough; a few presentation polish items remain (minor clarity improvements possible), but overall the writing is good and unambiguous.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important and practical bottleneck—creating realistic, diverse training corpora for NLG micro-planning—by proposing a semi-automatic pipeline (bigram+ILP content selection and crowdsourced verbalisation) and releasing DBPNLG, which the authors show is demonstrably more varied and challenging than RNNLG. From my methodology-oriented perspective, the dataset’s greater attribute/span variety, input-shape complexity, and the observed degradation of standard seq2seq models (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) signal a meaningful benchmark that should shift how researchers evaluate micro-planning approaches. The framework and empirical evidence open clear follow-on work (better models, alternative selection algorithms, reproducibility studies) and are likely to be adopted by the community, though the impact stops short of being transformative without code/data release and broader replication. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical bottleneck—training data for micro-planning—from KBs by proposing a semi-automatic extraction + crowdsourcing pipeline and releasing DBPNLG, which demonstrably yields richer, more varied inputs and harder generation targets than RNNLG. By showing standard seq2seq models struggle on this data (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) the work recalibrates expectations about current modelling limits and highlights the need for micro-planning–aware architectures. The framework and dataset are likely to be adopted by researchers studying content selection, ordering and lexicalisation from real KBs and to spur new lines of work on controllable generation, even if the dataset size and availability of code were not specified in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important and practical bottleneck—creating realistic, diverse training corpora for NLG micro-planning—by proposing a semi-automatic pipeline (bigram+ILP content selection and crowdsourced verbalisation) and releasing DBPNLG, which the authors show is demonstrably more varied and challenging than RNNLG. From my methodology-oriented perspective, the dataset’s greater attribute/span variety, input-shape complexity, and the observed degradation of standard seq2seq models (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) signal a meaningful benchmark that should shift how researchers evaluate micro-planning approaches. The framework and empirical evidence open clear follow-on work (better models, alternative selection algorithms, reproducibility studies) and are likely to be adopted by the community, though the impact stops short of being transformative without code/data release and broader replication.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical bottleneck—training data for micro-planning—from KBs by proposing a semi-automatic extraction + crowdsourcing pipeline and releasing DBPNLG, which demonstrably yields richer, more varied inputs and harder generation targets than RNNLG. By showing standard seq2seq models struggle on this data (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) the work recalibrates expectations about current modelling limits and highlights the need for micro-planning–aware architectures. The framework and dataset are likely to be adopted by researchers studying content selection, ordering and lexicalisation from real KBs and to spur new lines of work on controllable generation, even if the dataset size and availability of code were not specified in the summary.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides substantial empirical evidence that their semi-automatic pipeline yields a more diverse and challenging corpus than RNNLG: detailed corpus statistics (attributes, input shapes, lexical metrics) and downstream evaluation with a seq2seq baseline support the main claims. Related work coverage appears thorough (mentions WebNLG and other data-to-text efforts) and limitations are acknowledged. However, the experimental analysis lacks clear ablation/sensitivity studies of key components (e.g., impact of the different bigram models and the ILP objective) and no code release is mentioned, which hinders full reproducibility and makes it hard to verify some implementation choices and statistical robustness of the results. | B(4): The paper provides a substantial empirical evaluation: it constructs a new DBPNLG corpus with detailed corpus-level analyses (attributes, input shapes, lexical sophistication, CTTR, paraphrase counts), compares against RNNLG, and evaluates a standard seq2seq baseline with reported hyperparameters and automatic metrics (perplexity, BLEU). Related work appears well-covered (including prior crowdsourced and RDF-to-text efforts) and the authors acknowledge limitations of their pipeline and current neural models. However, the paper lacks clear ablation or sensitivity studies of core design choices (e.g., the contribution of different property bigram models or the ILP selection criteria), and reproducibility is weakened by no explicit statement about released code/data in the summary. Overall the experiments are solid and support the main claims but miss some analyses (ablations, additional baselines/human evaluation) that would make the evidence exhaustive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial empirical evidence that their semi-automatic pipeline yields a more diverse and challenging corpus than RNNLG: detailed corpus statistics (attributes, input shapes, lexical metrics) and downstream evaluation with a seq2seq baseline support the main claims. Related work coverage appears thorough (mentions WebNLG and other data-to-text efforts) and limitations are acknowledged. However, the experimental analysis lacks clear ablation/sensitivity studies of key components (e.g., impact of the different bigram models and the ILP objective) and no code release is mentioned, which hinders full reproducibility and makes it hard to verify some implementation choices and statistical robustness of the results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a substantial empirical evaluation: it constructs a new DBPNLG corpus with detailed corpus-level analyses (attributes, input shapes, lexical sophistication, CTTR, paraphrase counts), compares against RNNLG, and evaluates a standard seq2seq baseline with reported hyperparameters and automatic metrics (perplexity, BLEU). Related work appears well-covered (including prior crowdsourced and RDF-to-text efforts) and the authors acknowledge limitations of their pipeline and current neural models. However, the paper lacks clear ablation or sensitivity studies of core design choices (e.g., the contribution of different property bigram models or the ILP selection criteria), and reproducibility is weakened by no explicit statement about released code/data in the summary. Overall the experiments are solid and support the main claims but miss some analyses (ablations, additional baselines/human evaluation) that would make the evidence exhaustive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and defensible novelty: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines learned property bigram models with an ILP-based subtree selection mechanism applied to RDF KBs (DBpedia) together with a crowdsourcing validation loop to produce a micro-planning corpus (DBPNLG). While the overall goal (creating training corpora for RDF-to-text) overlaps with contemporaneous efforts such as WebNLG and earlier artificial-data crowdsourcing (Wen et al. 2016), the specific methodological combination (bigram sibling/chain interpolation + ILP for varied coherent data-unit extraction) and the empirical claim that DBPNLG is substantially more structurally and lexically diverse than RNNLG represent a non-trivial extension and clear differentiation from prior work. From an expert methodological perspective this is more than a straightforward reapplication of existing pipelines, though it is not a radical departure from the general space of RDF-to-text dataset construction—hence a solid but not revolutionary contribution. | B(4): This paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines learned property bigram models with an ILP-based subtree selector and a targeted crowdsourcing/validation process to produce DBPNLG from DBpedia. While related efforts such as WebNLG and RNNLG also produce RDF-to-text corpora, the paper’s specific micro-planning-focused selection algorithm and empirical analysis showing greater input-shape and lexical diversity (and correspondingly harder neural baselines) represent a non-trivial extension beyond prior dataset construction work. The methodological novelty (bigram+ILP for varied, coherent data-unit extraction) and the demonstrated challenge posed to standard seq2seq models would be informative to experts, even though the overall goal of creating RDF verbalisation corpora aligns with existing lines of work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and defensible novelty: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines learned property bigram models with an ILP-based subtree selection mechanism applied to RDF KBs (DBpedia) together with a crowdsourcing validation loop to produce a micro-planning corpus (DBPNLG). While the overall goal (creating training corpora for RDF-to-text) overlaps with contemporaneous efforts such as WebNLG and earlier artificial-data crowdsourcing (Wen et al. 2016), the specific methodological combination (bigram sibling/chain interpolation + ILP for varied coherent data-unit extraction) and the empirical claim that DBPNLG is substantially more structurally and lexically diverse than RNNLG represent a non-trivial extension and clear differentiation from prior work. From an expert methodological perspective this is more than a straightforward reapplication of existing pipelines, though it is not a radical departure from the general space of RDF-to-text dataset construction—hence a solid but not revolutionary contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines learned property bigram models with an ILP-based subtree selector and a targeted crowdsourcing/validation process to produce DBPNLG from DBpedia. While related efforts such as WebNLG and RNNLG also produce RDF-to-text corpora, the paper’s specific micro-planning-focused selection algorithm and empirical analysis showing greater input-shape and lexical diversity (and correspondingly harder neural baselines) represent a non-trivial extension beyond prior dataset construction work. The methodological novelty (bigram+ILP for varied, coherent data-unit extraction) and the demonstrated challenge posed to standard seq2seq models would be informative to experts, even though the overall goal of creating RDF verbalisation corpora aligns with existing lines of work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its claims and provides empirical evidence (corpus statistics and seq2seq evaluation) showing DBPNLG is more diverse and challenging than RNNLG. The methodology appears well-engineered (property bigram models, ILP subtree selection, crowdsourcing and validation) and hyperparameters are reported, but experimental rigour is somewhat limited: comparisons rely primarily on a single seq2seq baseline, no statistical significance testing is reported, and code release is not mentioned which hampers reproducibility. I also note a modest overreach in arguing that the Wen et al. crowdsourcing approach "cannot" be used to train KB verbalizers without stronger evidence. Overall the results support the main conclusions, but some methodological gaps and missing controls keep this from being technically flawless. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by the described pipeline, corpus statistics, and empirical comparisons showing higher complexity and lower seq2seq performance on DBPNLG relative to RNNLG. Mathematical content is limited (ILP selection and bigram models) and appears plausible; no derivations are required. The experimental methodology is reasonable (appropriate metrics and a seq2seq baseline), but relies on a single neural baseline and lacks mention of statistical significance tests or additional modern baselines, which weakens the strength of some empirical claims. Overall the conclusions about DBPNLG being a more challenging benchmark are justified, though some claims (e.g., unsuitability of prior crowdsourced data for KB verbalisation) would benefit from broader baseline comparisons or ablation analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its claims and provides empirical evidence (corpus statistics and seq2seq evaluation) showing DBPNLG is more diverse and challenging than RNNLG. The methodology appears well-engineered (property bigram models, ILP subtree selection, crowdsourcing and validation) and hyperparameters are reported, but experimental rigour is somewhat limited: comparisons rely primarily on a single seq2seq baseline, no statistical significance testing is reported, and code release is not mentioned which hampers reproducibility. I also note a modest overreach in arguing that the Wen et al. crowdsourcing approach "cannot" be used to train KB verbalizers without stronger evidence. Overall the results support the main conclusions, but some methodological gaps and missing controls keep this from being technically flawless.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by the described pipeline, corpus statistics, and empirical comparisons showing higher complexity and lower seq2seq performance on DBPNLG relative to RNNLG. Mathematical content is limited (ILP selection and bigram models) and appears plausible; no derivations are required. The experimental methodology is reasonable (appropriate metrics and a seq2seq baseline), but relies on a single neural baseline and lacks mention of statistical significance tests or additional modern baselines, which weakens the strength of some empirical claims. Overall the conclusions about DBPNLG being a more challenging benchmark are justified, though some claims (e.g., unsuitability of prior crowdsourced data for KB verbalisation) would benefit from broader baseline comparisons or ablation analyses.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it addresses data-to-text micro-planning, proposes a reproducible semi-automatic pipeline (property bigram models + ILP + crowdsourcing) and introduces a new dataset (DBPNLG) contrasted rigorously with RNNLG. The problem formulation, use of ILP for content selection, and experimental comparisons (perplexity, BLEU, detailed corpus statistics and lexical measures) meet community standards for methods and evaluation; hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough. My main minor concern is that code release is not mentioned, which would strengthen reproducibility, but this does not detract from its clear relevance and strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper squarely targets the NLG/data-to-text community and presents a clearly relevant contribution (DBPNLG) for micro-planning research by mining DBpedia and combining probabilistic sibling/chain models, an ILP selector, and a validated crowdsourcing verbalisation pipeline. The methods and evaluations use community-expected standards (comparison to RNNLG, seq2seq baselines, a wide set of linguistic and generation metrics such as CTTR, LS, perplexity, BLEU) and the analysis demonstrably shows DBPNLG is a more challenging, diverse benchmark. Given its focus on resource creation, annotation quality, and evaluation for NLG modelling, the paper is well positioned for a top-tier NLP venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it addresses data-to-text micro-planning, proposes a reproducible semi-automatic pipeline (property bigram models + ILP + crowdsourcing) and introduces a new dataset (DBPNLG) contrasted rigorously with RNNLG. The problem formulation, use of ILP for content selection, and experimental comparisons (perplexity, BLEU, detailed corpus statistics and lexical measures) meet community standards for methods and evaluation; hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough. My main minor concern is that code release is not mentioned, which would strengthen reproducibility, but this does not detract from its clear relevance and strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper squarely targets the NLG/data-to-text community and presents a clearly relevant contribution (DBPNLG) for micro-planning research by mining DBpedia and combining probabilistic sibling/chain models, an ILP selector, and a validated crowdsourcing verbalisation pipeline. The methods and evaluations use community-expected standards (comparison to RNNLG, seq2seq baselines, a wide set of linguistic and generation metrics such as CTTR, LS, perplexity, BLEU) and the analysis demonstrably shows DBPNLG is a more challenging, diverse benchmark. Given its focus on resource creation, annotation quality, and evaluation for NLG modelling, the paper is well positioned for a top-tier NLP venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus against the widely-used RNNLG dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence neural baseline on both, which gives a useful, controlled comparison showing DBPNLG is more challenging. However, empirical comparisons are limited: only a single neural baseline (Vinyals et al.) is used and no alternative micro-planning or WebNLG-style systems, template-based or pipeline baselines, or recent data-to-text models are evaluated. The authors do situate their work relative to prior corpus construction efforts (Wen et al., WebNLG and other datasets) and analyze dataset-vs-dataset differences in detail, but the experimental section would be stronger with additional, fair baselines and ablations to demonstrate the practical impact on micro-planning methods. | B(4): The paper compares its new DBPNLG resource against a prominent crowdsourced benchmark (RNNLG) and evaluates a standard seq2seq with attention baseline, using relevant metrics (perplexity, BLEU, lexical and structural measures). Comparisons appear fair in that the same evaluation metrics are used and limitations of prior crowdsourcing (Wen et al.) are discussed. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other contemporary RDF-to-text benchmarks and systems (notably WebNLG and its participating models) which are directly relevant for micro-planning from RDF, so a few important baselines/benchmarks are missing despite thorough related-work discussion.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus against the widely-used RNNLG dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence neural baseline on both, which gives a useful, controlled comparison showing DBPNLG is more challenging. However, empirical comparisons are limited: only a single neural baseline (Vinyals et al.) is used and no alternative micro-planning or WebNLG-style systems, template-based or pipeline baselines, or recent data-to-text models are evaluated. The authors do situate their work relative to prior corpus construction efforts (Wen et al., WebNLG and other datasets) and analyze dataset-vs-dataset differences in detail, but the experimental section would be stronger with additional, fair baselines and ablations to demonstrate the practical impact on micro-planning methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its new DBPNLG resource against a prominent crowdsourced benchmark (RNNLG) and evaluates a standard seq2seq with attention baseline, using relevant metrics (perplexity, BLEU, lexical and structural measures). Comparisons appear fair in that the same evaluation metrics are used and limitations of prior crowdsourcing (Wen et al.) are discussed. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other contemporary RDF-to-text benchmarks and systems (notably WebNLG and its participating models) which are directly relevant for micro-planning from RDF, so a few important baselines/benchmarks are missing despite thorough related-work discussion.

main_claims: A novel semi-automatic framework to create data-to-text training corpora for NLG micro-planning from existing RDF knowledge bases (illustrated on DBPedia).
The framework combines a content-selection module (bigram models + ILP) to extract varied, coherent data units and a crowdsourcing pipeline to collect and validate human verbalisations.

The resulting DBPNLG dataset is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG dataset despite being smaller, exhibiting more attributes, more input shapes, greater lexical variety and longer/more complex texts.
Standard sequence-to-sequence neural models perform worse (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) on DBPNLG than on RNNLG, showing DBPNLG is a more challenging benchmark for learning micro-planning.
_summary.methodology: Extract category-specific entity graphs from DBPedia, learn property bigram models (sibling, chain, interpolated), select subtrees via an ILP formulation to produce data units of varying size/shape, then collect and validate multiple human verbalisations per data unit using crowdsourcing with automatic and manual quality checks.
datasets: Mentioned: domain-specific datasets (Chen and Mooney 2008, Liang et al. 2009, Ratnaparkhi 2000, KBGen 2013), SR’11, AMR corpus
baselines: Sequence-to-sequence model with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015) (used as comparative model)
limitations: The crowdsourcing approach of Wen et al. (2016) uses artificial data and thus cannot be used to train KB verbalisers.
Sequence-to-sequence models are reported as not optimal for generating linguistically complex texts from rich data (i.e., current neural models struggle on DBPNLG).

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems, specializing in experimental design, reproducibility, and robust evaluation of data-driven models. Has led reproducibility efforts and benchmark construction in several machine learning and NLP projects.
traits: Insists on clear baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance testing to support empirical claims
Looks for complete implementation details (hyperparameters, training/validation splits, random seeds) and wants code/data release for reproducibility
Evaluates evaluation: questions choice of metrics, corpus sampling, and whether reported improvements are meaningful and robust
Notices data preprocessing choices, potential leakage, and whether comparisons are apples-to-apples (model capacity, training time, data size)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Systematic and detail-oriented: focuses on experimental rigor, reproducibility, and whether the empirical evidence supports the paper's claims.

reviewer_b.name: NLG Domain Expert
reviewer_b.background: Established researcher in natural language generation and data-to-text with extensive experience in dataset creation, micro-planning, and evaluation of generation systems. Has published and reviewed work on NLG benchmarks, annotation pipelines, and linguistic analysis of corpora.
traits: Assesses novelty relative to prior dataset construction and micro-planning literature and whether the contribution addresses a genuine gap
Values annotation quality, linguistic diversity, and whether dataset design supports downstream research needs (e.g., varied input shapes, controllability)
Considers broader impact: community adoption potential, dataset utility for modelling micro-planning phenomena, and clarity of licensing/availability
Attends to qualitative analysis of outputs, examples, and error modes rather than only aggregate metrics
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Big-picture and constructive: situates the work within the research landscape, judges its potential impact, and recommends practical improvements to increase utility and adoption.
